In Latin, the first words of today's psalm. Psalm 133. Ece quam bonum. Behold, how good. E.Q.B.<|17.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That might seem a little weird, and I really didn't just randomly pick that from scripture,<|5.76|> <|5.76|> I promise.<|7.08|> <|7.08|> It is the motto of my alma mater of Sewanee, and it appears on the university seal and<|15.54|> <|15.54|> is often referenced as a touchstone for the community that Sewanee hopes to foster.<|23.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "Behold how good and pleasant it is when kindred dwell together in unity."<|9.00|> <|9.00|> It's an aspirational motto for sure.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> We sometimes fail, we often succeed, but we continually make efforts.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> Behold how good.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> good. And this is the perfect psalm for this second Sunday of Easter. It evokes many of the<|9.80|> <|9.80|> stories and images that we've just lived through on our journey to Easter.<|17.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This day when we still vividly remember that Jesus created an entirely new family for his mother and the beloved disciple from the cross.<|12.96|> <|12.96|> When we are still basking in the return of Alleluia and still savoring that sweet, sugary taste of jelly beans and Peeps.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I know peeps are a source of division for us.<|4.72|> <|4.88|> I happen to love them, especially the pink ones.<|8.82|> <|9.86|> It is a psalm of hope and promise.<|13.98|> <|14.74|> It is a psalm praising the very best parts of community,<|21.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The kindred of whom the psalmist writes are not blood relatives, but rather a people joined by faith and by God's grace.<|13.20|> <|14.04|> Like John and Mary are joined from the cross.<|18.74|> <|19.46|> Like the disciples who are huddled together in today's gospel reading.<|24.68|> <|25.24|> Like all of us.<|27.70|> Expensive and fragrant nard poured over him as she sat at his feet.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My liturgics professor in seminary was adamant that we not be stingy with oil at baptisms.<|11.52|> <|11.52|> He is of the belief that it is meant to be used in abundance and that anointing should even be a little messy.<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Here, on Sunday mornings, you get a fairly tame baptism, but we have had some that have<|9.60|> <|9.60|> become the thing of legend.<|13.66|> the font and pour<|8.96|> <|8.96|> on the baby's head, and one full of chrism, that oil with which we put the sign of the<|18.16|> <|18.16|> cross on heads in here.<|21.24|> <|21.24|> Well, we were supposed to take that little pitcher of oil and just drip, repeat after<|27.62|> <|27.62|> me, drip.<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> drip oil on the baby's head as we could like the baptisms we will see shortly,<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> was a welcoming into community,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> an invitation to dwell with us in unity.<|12.00|>
